gauge.name: French Broad River at Rosman

impacts.statement: Major flooding continues.  Extremely rare and catastrophic flooding of the French Broad River is ongoing with disastrous impacts to property, including within the town of Rosman.  Flood waters may surround the water treatment plant and power substation near the French Broad River.

Major Flood Stage.  Extremely rare and catastrophic flooding of the French Broad River is developing with disastrous impacts to property.  Extensive flooding of homes and businesses between US 178 and Main St is developing.  Basements near the river will be inundated with at least 4-7 feet of water.  Homes along E Depot St will see inundation up to 2 feet deep.  Floodwater levels will be similar to those that occurred in August 2021 during Tropical Cyclone Fred.

Moderate flooding continues.  Extensive flooding of homes and businesses between US 178 and Main St is developing.  Basements near the river will be inundated with at least 2-4 feet of water.  Floodwaters will begin entering homes along E Depot St.  Significant flooding of upstream commercial property near the confluence of the N Fork and W Fork French Broad rivers is developing.  Floodwater levels will be similar to those that occurred in August 2021 immediately preceding Tropical Cyclone Fred.

Moderate Flood Stage.  Unusual, damaging flooding is developing adjacent to the French Broad River with considerable impacts to property and travel.  Significant flooding reaching 4-6 ft deep is ongoing for low-lying areas and low-water crossings.  Secondary roads, outbuildings, crawl spaces, and/or basements may be experiencing flooding reaching 2-4 feet deep, including along Chestnut St, Main St, and US 178.  Some homes and businesses may be stranded.  Significant impacts to travel are likely.

Minor flooding continues.  The French Broad River is approaching homes along Depot St. in Rosman likely requiring evacuations.  Additionally, floodwaters will inundate US 178 adjacent to the gauge site and require closure.
Minor flooding continues.  Extensive flooding of low-lying secondary roads, including E Depot St, Hannah Ford Rd, Island Ford Rd, and Green Rd, is ongoing.

Minor Flood Stage.  Common, deeper flooding of low-lying areas adjacent to the French Broad River is developing with lesser impacts to vulnerable property.  More significant floodwaters are beginning to impact low-lying areas, with inundation approximately 2-4 ft deep.  Adjacent campgrounds, secondary roads, and outbuildings may be experiencing flooding up to 1-2 ft deep.  Flooding less than 1 ft deep may begin impacting basements and primary roads, including E. Main St. and Depot St.

Action Stage/Advisory flooding continues.  Widespread flooding of adjacent low-lying areas is ongoing along the upper French Broad River.  Hannah Ford, Island Ford, and Green Rds are experiencing flooding and are likely impassible.

Action/Advisory Stage.  Common, nuisance flooding of low-lying areas adjacent to the French Broad River is beginning.  Hannah Ford, Island Ford, and Green Rds may begin to flood with up to 1 foot of inundation.  Floodwaters are beginning to impact farmland, woodlands, nurseries, parks, athletic fields, golf courses, greenways, and boat-access areas with up to 2 feet of inundation.  Low-water crossings may be inundated with up to 2 feet of floodwater and become impassable.

value.label: National Geodetic Vertical Datum of 1929
value.description: A fixed reference adopted as a standard geodetic datum for elevations determined by leveling. The datum was derived for surveys from a general adjustment of the first-order leveling nets of both the United States and Canada. In the adjustment, mean sea level was held fixed as observed at 21 tide stations in the United States and 5 in Canada. The year indicates the time of the general adjustment. A synonym for Sea-level Datum of 1929. The geodetic datum is fixed and does not take into account the changing stands of sea level. Because there are many variables affecting sea level, and because the geodetic datum represents a best fit over a broad area, the relationship between the geodetic datum and local mean sea level is not consistent from one location to another in either time or space. For this reason, the National Geodetic Vertical Datum should not be confused with mean sea level. See North American Vertical Datum of 1988 (NAVD 88). NGVD 29 should not be used as Mean Sea Level. NGVD 29 is no longer supported by NGS.

gauge.forecastReliability: Forecasts are not available. Only observed stages are available for this point.
